title: Te Aho Tapu o te Awa: The Sacred Thread of the River - Weaving Arts with Environmental Kaitiakitanga
subject: Arts
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway that flows through our rohe. According to Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau, the awa was created by the tears of Papatūānuku when she was separated from Ranginui. Local kaumātua speak of how the stream's patterns mirror traditional weaving patterns, particularly the poutama (stairway to heaven) design, representing our journey as kaitiaki. The stream's health has declined due to urban development, making this both an artistic and environmental restoration project.
learningObjectives: Create an original artwork using natural materials from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku that visually communicates the stream's current state and cultural significance
Analyze and document at least three traditional Māori weaving patterns found in the stream's natural formations and explain their cultural meanings
Collaborate with local kaumātua to incorporate authentic Ngāti Kahungunu stories and perspectives into artistic representations
Develop and present a proposal for how art can be used as a tool for environmental advocacy and community education about the awa

activities.title: Whakatau ki te Awa - Ceremonial Welcome to the Stream
activities.description: Begin with a karakia led by local kaumātua at the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students will learn the pūrākau of the stream, make visual observations of its current state, and collect natural materials (with permission and following tikanga) for later art creation while documenting the experience through sketching and photography.

activities.title: Te Aho Tapu - The Sacred Thread Weaving Workshop
activities.description: Working with local weavers from Ngāti Kahungunu, students learn basic weaving techniques using harakeke and other natural materials. They explore how traditional patterns (kōwhaiwhai, tukutuku) reflect environmental patterns and create small woven pieces that tell the story of the awa's journey.

activities.title: Pūrākau Paints - Storytelling Through Earth Pigments
activities.description: Students create natural pigments from soils and plants gathered from the awa's banks. Using these pigments, they create artworks on recycled materials that depict both the historical pūrākau of the stream and its current environmental challenges, blending traditional storytelling with contemporary environmental messaging.

activities.title: Te Kōrero o te Awa - Voice of the River Installation
activities.description: Working in groups, students create an interactive art installation using collected natural materials, recorded sounds of the stream, and digital elements. The installation must communicate both the cultural significance and environmental status of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, inviting viewers to reflect on their role as kaitiaki.

resources: Local Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and weavers (specific contacts: Whaea Rina Smith and Matua Hone Te Kani)
Digital archive 'Ngā Pūrākau o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku' available through Hawke's Bay Knowledge Bank
Natural material collection kits (approved by local iwi environmental committee)
Documentary 'Te Huarahi o te Wai: The Water's Path' featuring local environmental artists

tasks: Creation of a portfolio documenting the artistic process from material collection to final artwork
Oral presentation explaining the cultural and environmental messages in their artwork to community members
Written reflection connecting their art practice to concepts of kaitiakitanga and environmental stewardship
Peer assessment of collaborative installation project using co-created rubrics

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Level 4 - Investigate the purpose of objects and images from past and present cultures and identify the contexts in which they were or are made, viewed, and valued
Developing Practical Knowledge in the Arts: Level 4 - Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and selected principles through the use of materials and processes
Developing Ideas in the Arts: Level 4 - Develop and refine ideas in selected art disciplines in response to a variety of motivations, observation, and imagination
Communicating and Interpreting in the Arts: Level 4 - Describe how ideas and art-making processes are used to communicate meaning in selected objects and images
